claim: The attribution UNIT is the individual STEP within an execution trace (modeled as sequential chains of dependent steps), not a discrete workflow-graph node/agent/module/tool-call component. The method does not explicitly map step-level blame up to agent/node-level blocks, making it primarily step-granularity rather than node-granularity.
quote: CausalFlow models execution traces as sequential chains of dependent steps

output.evidence:
PRIMARY SOURCE CONFIRMS the claim. arXiv 2605.25338 "CausalFlow: Causal Attribution and Counterfactual Repair for LLM Agent Failures." Abstract (verbatim, matches the supporting quote): CausalFlow "models execution traces as sequential chains of dependent steps and computes Causal Responsibility Scores (CRS) via step-level counterfactual intervention to identify failure-inducing steps." So the attribution UNIT is the individual step in a linear trace, confirming Part 1.

A "step" is defined as "each step s_t contains an action, such as a reasoning token sequence or tool call, and, when applicable, an environment observation," with six types: REASONING, TOOL_CALL, TOOL_RESPONSE, LLM_RESPONSE, MEMORY_ACCESS, FINAL_ANSWER. Note a step can BE a tool call, but it is still treated as a position in a sequential chain, not as a distinct workflow-graph node with topology — this reinforces (does not refute) the "step-granularity not node-granularity" characterization.

Part 2 (no step->node/agent rollup) CONFIRMED across two independent fetches: paper explicitly scopes to "single-agent sequential traces without requiring framework modification or checkpointing infrastructure" and contrasts with multi-agent orchestrator/sub-agent work (DoVer). No agent-level/module-level/node-level aggregation of CRS exists; repairs operate per single step, producing (wrong step, corrected step) contrastive pairs.

REFUTATION ATTEMPT (tool-call angle): the claim says "not a tool-call component," yet TOOL_CALL is a valid step type — but CausalFlow attributes to the step-position-in-chain, treating tool calls as one step type among six in a linear trace, NOT as discrete graph nodes. This nuance supports, not contradicts, the claim. Source is primary (paper abstract + HTML body), verbatim, current (2026), non-marketing. Claim is accurately supported.

claim: The method produces actionable localization: a precise root-cause report feeds an optimization agent whose causal-guided repairs raised end-to-end task success from 15.4% to 37.8% (+22.4 points), exceeding a random-repair control (18.3%), demonstrating closed-loop conservative node-level fixing of an existing workflow.
quote: Repairs guided by our causal analysis boosted the overall task success rate from 15.4% to 37.8%—an absolute improvement of 22.4 percentage points.

output.evidence:
VERIFIED in primary source (arXiv 2509.08682v1, "Automatic Failure Attribution and Critical Step Prediction Method for Multi-Agent Systems Based on Causal Inference," Ma et al., Sept 2025). Every number in the claim is confirmed verbatim: Table 2 "Overall" row shows Base SR = 15.4, Rand. SR = 18.3, Causal SR = 37.8; text reads verbatim "Repairs guided by our causal analysis boosted the overall task success rate from 15.4% to 37.8%—an absolute improvement of 22.4 percentage points." The optimization loop is real: "An optimization agent, powered by GPT-4o, then used this targeted diagnosis to automatically generate a revised solution," over N=50 failed Who&When trajectories. The 18.3% random-repair control (which the task's own supporting quote did NOT include) is genuinely present in the paper, not a fabrication. The paper exists, is not retracted, and is corroborated by sibling 2025 causal-attribution work (A2P/2509.10401, RAFFLES/2509.06822). So the claim is NOT unsupported, NOT contradicted, NOT outdated (Sept 2025 = current SOTA), and the source is the appropriate primary one for a descriptive "the paper demonstrates X" claim.

HOWEVER, the claim should be DOWN-WEIGHTED with these flags (refutation attempts that landed as qualifications, not kills): (1) Self-reported single-source benchmark with NO independent replication. (2) Evaluation method for "success" is UNSPECIFIED in the paper ("re-executed... and the new overall task success rate was measured") — given GPT-4o performs diagnosis AND repair generation, there is an unaddressed self-evaluation/circularity risk. (3) The "random repair" control is under-specified ("a random, non-targeted modification") undermining baseline comparability. (4) NO significance test, variance, or CI on the N=50 point estimates (the repair experiment, unlike the ablation, reports only point estimates). (5) "conservative node-level fixing" is the CLAIMANT'S editorial gloss — the paper attributes at agent-level (Shapley) and step-level (CDC-MAS) and repairs at the step level, but never characterizes the repairs as "conservative"/minimal-edit; an optimization agent regenerating a "revised solution" and re-executing is not established as a minimal intervention. The factual core survives refutation; the editorializations ("conservative," "end-to-end") slightly overstate.
output.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Refutation attempts targeted internal rigor: arXiv 2509.08682v1 itself omits (a) the success-evaluation mechanism, (b) a precise random-baseline definition, and (c) any significance/variance for Table 2's N=50 point estimates; and the claim's words "conservative" and "node-level" are claimant editorializations not used by the paper. These weaken confidence but do not contradict the verified numbers.

claim: A2P is a counterfactual/causal-intervention attribution algorithm operationalizing Pearl's structural causal model: it (1) Abducts hidden root causes behind an agent's action, (2) Acts by defining a minimal corrective intervention via the do()-operator, and (3) Predicts by simulating the counterfactual trajectory (3-5 turns) to verify whether correcting that single action would have averted the failure — distinguishing decisive errors from incidental ones. It runs within a single inference pass as an enhancement of the All-at-Once method (retaining full-context access).
quote: A2P guides an LLM through a formal, three-step counterfactual reasoning process within a single inference pass, operationalizing the logic of Pearl's structural causal model hierarchy ... (1) Abduct, inferring hidden factors ... (2) Act, defining a minimal, concrete corrective intervention; and (3) Predict, simulating the subsequent counterfactual trajectory to verify if the intervention would have resolved the overall task failure ... Our approach is implemented as an enhancement to the All-at-Once method, thereby retaining its key advantage of having access to the complete conversational context.

output.evidence:
Verified verbatim against the primary source (arXiv:2509.10401v2, PDF text-extracted). EVERY clause of the claim is directly supported:

(1) "operationalizing Pearl's SCM": §1 intro states A2P guides an LLM "operationalizing the logic of Pearl's structural causal model hierarchy (Pearl, 2009)"; §3 "We formalize the failure attribution task within Pearl's SCM framework."

(2) Abduct hidden root causes: "(1) Abduct, inferring hidden factors (e.g., a flawed assumption) that explain a problematic action"; "establish a plausible root cause before proceeding."

(3) Act = minimal corrective intervention via do()-operator: VERBATIM — "the framework prompts the LLM to define a minimal, concrete intervention. This corresponds to applying the do()-operator in Pearl's causal calculus (Pearl et al., 2016)." Eq.2: "Action: do(a_t → a'_t)."

(4) Predict = simulate counterfactual trajectory 3-5 turns: VERBATIM — "(3) Prediction (Simulating the Counterfactual Trajectory)... The LLM is instructed to simulate the subsequent 3-5 turns of the conversation under the counterfactual condition." Figure 2 confirms "flexible 3-5 step range."

(5) Distinguishes decisive vs incidental: VERBATIM — "essential for distinguishing decisive errors from incidental mistakes."

(6) Single inference pass / enhancement of All-at-Once / full context: VERBATIM — "three-step counterfactual reasoning process within a single inference pass"; "implemented as an enhancement to the All-at-Once method, thereby retaining its key advantage of having access to the complete conversational context."

The claim's own supporting quote is a faithful, lightly-trimmed excerpt of the real paper. The claim is purely DESCRIPTIVE of the method's design (not an empirical/superiority assertion), so it requires no external corroboration. Skeptical checks: no overreach (claim preserves the paper's own hedges "approximates"/"minimal"/"operationalizing the logic of"); current (Sept 2025); "turns" matches the prose sentence paraphrased ("turn"/"step" used interchangeably, each turn prefixed "Step {idx}"). 

One genuine red flag found and weighed: the paper carries a disclaimer that "ours DeepScientist system performed approximately 95% of the work... approach this work with caution" — i.e., it is largely AI-authored. However, this caveat bears on the paper's EMPIRICAL claims (the 47.46%/29.31% accuracy figures, novelty), which the claim under review does NOT invoke. The descriptive method-summary claim remains an accurate restatement of the authors' own algorithm description. Could not refute.
output.counterSource: No contradicting source exists. Searched WebSearch ("A2P Abduct Act Predict failure attribution"); only hits are the paper itself (arXiv abs/html/pdf), its ResearchGate mirror, the GitHub repo ResearAI/A2P, and the parent DeepScientist paper (arXiv:2509.26603) — none dispute the method description. The sole quality caveat is internal: the paper's own AI-authorship disclaimer, which targets empirical results not the design description under review.

claim: CausalFlow performs failure attribution by computing 'Causal Responsibility Scores' (CRS) via step-level counterfactual intervention to identify the specific failure-inducing step within a trace. This is a causal/counterfactual (interventional) attribution algorithm, directly relevant to counterfactual blame-assignment approaches.
quote: CausalFlow models execution traces as sequential chains of dependent steps and computes Causal Responsibility Scores(CRS) via step-level counterfactual intervention to identify failure-inducing steps.

output.evidence:
VERIFIED, claim accurate and not overstated. Primary source arXiv:2605.25338 "CausalFlow: Causal Attribution and Counterfactual Repair for LLM Agent Failures" (Bonagiri, Borkar, Anderias, Rafatirad, Homayoun, UC Davis) is a real paper. The supporting quote is taken VERBATIM from the abstract. Every factual element checks out: (1) CRS is computed via step-level counterfactual intervention — the formula is CRS(s_i)=max_{k∈1..K} 𝕀[V(y(τ[i←s_i'(k)]),x)=1], i.e. replace a candidate step, re-execute downstream, and assign CRS=1 if any intervention flips the verifier from failure→success; (2) it identifies "failure-inducing steps" within a trace; (3) it IS a genuine causal/counterfactual (interventional) algorithm — the intervene-and-replay-downstream mechanism is the canonical counterfactual-replay technique named in the research question's Core Question 3 ("counterfactual replay of a node"). Confirmed across 2 independent WebFetch calls + 3 WebSearch calls; no contradicting source found.

IMPORTANT CAVEAT (does NOT refute the claim, but matters for report framing): CausalFlow operates on SINGLE-AGENT sequential traces, NOT multi-agent systems, and its attribution unit is the individual trace STEP (typed REASONING / TOOL_CALL / TOOL_RESPONSE / LLM_RESPONSE / MEMORY_ACCESS / FINAL_ANSWER; a step = "a reasoning token sequence or tool call"), NOT a workflow-graph node or agent. It does NOT bridge step-level signals up to node/agent-level blame. Benchmarks are GSM8K, MBPP, SealQA-Hard, MedBrowseComp (>3,000 problems; converts 42.7% of failed executions into validated minimal repairs) — all single-agent. Per the research question's exclusion rule (down-weight pure CoT-step work "with no node/agent mapping"), CausalFlow sits at the boundary: its TOOL_CALL steps are node-like ("tool-call step" is explicitly in-scope per the question), but its REASONING steps are CoT-granularity. CRITICALLY, the claim as written does NOT overclaim node/agent granularity — it explicitly and correctly scopes to "step-level... step within a trace" and only asserts relevance to "counterfactual blame-assignment approaches," which is true. No overreach. Note: the search surfaced DISTINCT multi-agent papers that should not be conflated with CausalFlow (arXiv:2602.23701 hierarchical MAS attribution; 2509.08682 critical-step causal inference; 2509.03312 AgenTracer; 2512.06749 DoVer; 2606.00765 FALAT). Minor: arxiv ID 2605 = May 2026 is future-dated relative to a normal calendar but consistent with the stated current date 2026-06-19 and the field's pace; the paper resolves and is real, so not a fabrication red flag.
output.counterSource: No contradicting source exists; the supporting quote is verbatim from the arXiv:2605.25338 abstract and corroborated by arxiv.org/html and arxiv.org/pdf full text. The only qualification (single-agent step-level, not multi-agent node-level) comes from the paper's own body and refines report framing rather than contradicting the claim, which already correctly says "step within a trace."

claim: DoVer's intervention-based repair produces measurable outcome improvements: within the Magnetic-One agent framework it flips 18% (AssistantBench) and 28% (GAIA) of previously failed trials into successes and validates or refutes 30-60% of failure hypotheses, and it generalizes to a different framework (AG2) and dataset (GSMPlus) with a 49% trial flip rate (~27.5% reported on GSMPlus trials in Table 2).
quote: DoVer recovers 18% and 28% of failures on datasets from AssistantBench Yoran et al. (2024) and GAIA Mialon et al. (2024), respectively. It further enables the validation or refutation of 30-60% of failure hypotheses, depending on task complexity. ... DoVer again performs effectively, achieving a 49% flip rate on failure cases.

output.evidence:
The claim is mostly supported but contains a specific, verifiable FACTUAL ERROR in its parenthetical, which I confirmed twice against the primary source (arxiv.org/html/2512.06749v3, Table 2).

CONTRADICTED PART: The claim states "a 49% trial flip rate (~27.5% reported on GSMPlus trials in Table 2)." This is FALSE and internally contradictory. In Table 2, GSMPlus shows a Trial Success Rate of 49.0% — NOT 27.5%. The 27.5% value belongs exclusively to a DIFFERENT row, GAIA-Level-1. The paper's own sentence confirms: "in the GSMPlus setting, DoVer achieves nearly a 50% trial success rate." The claim misreads the table, conflating GAIA-Level-1 (27.5%) with GSMPlus (49.0%), and then self-contradicts by citing both 49% and 27.5% for the same GSMPlus row.

SUPPORTED PARTS (for completeness): The other figures check out against the source. Abstract: "DoVer flips 18-28% of failed trials into successes" / "recovers 18% and 28% of failures on datasets from AssistantBench... and GAIA" (Table 2: AssistantBench WW-AB = 17.6% ≈ 18%; the 28% maps to GAIA-Level-1, which the paper itself labels under GAIA). "validates or refutes 30-60% of failure hypotheses" — verbatim correct. GSMPlus/AG2 generalization: "achieving a 49% flip rate on failure cases" and AG2+GSMPlus explicitly framed as "another agent framework... using a different dataset" — correct. Frameworks Magnetic-One (AssistantBench/GAIA) and AG2 (GSMPlus) — correct.

Source quality is high (primary arXiv source, Microsoft/CAS authors), and the bulk of the claim is current and well-supported. But the claim asserts a concrete falsifiable number ("~27.5% reported on GSMPlus trials in Table 2") that is flatly wrong against the cited table — GSMPlus is 49.0%, and 27.5% is GAIA-Level-1. Per the rubric, a claim containing a demonstrable misread of its own primary source is an overreach and is refuted.
output.counterSource: https://arxiv.org/html/2512.06749v3 (Table 2: GSMPlus Trial Success Rate = 49.0%; GAIA-Level-1 = 27.5%)

claim: AgenTracer attributes failure at the agent/step level: it localizes both the responsible AGENT and the 'decisive error' step within a multi-agent execution trace, defined as the earliest agent-step pair (i*, t*) whose correction flips the system from failure to success. quote: Pinpointing the specific agent or step responsible for an error within long execution traces defines the task of agentic system failure attribution. ... the earliest agent-step pair whose correction is sufficient to steer the system from failure to success — formally (i*, t*) = arg min_{(i,t)∈C(τ)} t

output.evidence: Claim FULLY SUPPORTED by primary source (AgenTracer, arXiv 2509.03312 / "Who Is Inducing Failure in the LLM Agentic Systems?"). (3) Formula confirmed: paper defines decisive agent-step pair set C(τ) = {(i,t) | i=ψ(t) and Φ(τ)=1...(R)=0}, then Eq.5 "(i*,t*) = arg min t" selecting "the earliest error in time." (4) Outputs BOTH agent and step verbatim: "the goal of our failure tracer, AgenTracer, is to take a failed trajectory τ as input and output the failure-responsible agent i* and the decisive error step t*." (5) Node/agent-level (not CoT-scoring) is correct: uses counterfactual replay (oracle rectification operator R re-simulating subsequent steps) over multi-agent systems (MetaGPT, MaAS, Magnetic-One, AG2) where an "agent" is a discrete workflow component; i* = ψ(t*) maps the step to its acting agent. Adversarial checks all fail to refute: quote is real and verbatim; source is primary (paper itself, corroborated by emergentmind, HuggingFace papers, ResearchGate); date Sept 2025 = current SOTA; not marketing (these are the task-definition equations). (1) "All-at-once: An LLM is provided with a query and the complete failure log, and it is tasked with identifying the failure-responsible agent as well as the specific step where the decisive error occurred." -> matches "whole-trace single query."
(2) "Step-by-step: ... the failure log is presented step-by-step. At each step, the LLM is tasked with determining whether a mistake has occurred in the current step. (2) "Step {idx} - Agent_Name:" pre-processing — CONFIRMED verbatim: "Before being passed to the model, the entire conversation log is pre-processed to prefix each turn with an explicit, formatted identifier like Step {idx} - Agent_Name:" (lines 247-248). claim: The core causal mechanism is the Performance Causal Inversion (PCI) principle: data-dependency edges observed in execution logs must be reversed in direction to build a correct performance causal graph, because information flow runs opposite to performance causation.

output.evidence: Verified verbatim against the primary source by extracting the downloaded PDF of arXiv 2509.08682 ("Automatic Failure Attribution and Critical Step Prediction Method for Multi-Agent Systems Based on Causal Inference," Ma et al., 10 Sep 2025) with PyMuPDF. Every component of the claim is confirmed: (1) The paper explicitly coins the term: "we propose the Performance Causal Inversion (PCI) principle: to construct an accurate performance causal graph, the data dependencies observed from the execution logs must be reversed in direction." (3) The reasoning the claim describes matches exactly: upstream A transmits data to downstream B (data flow A->B), but if A provides erroneous data causing B's failure, "A's performance state is the root cause of B's performance manifestation," so causal edges reverse the data edges (Definition 1: E_data={(v_i,v_j)|output(v_i) subset input(v_j)} -> inverted to E_causal). Caveat (non-refuting): this is one paper's proposed methodological principle, not an independently validated universal law, but the claim is correctly scoped as "the core causal mechanism" of this specific method, so source strength matches claim strength.

This makes its attribution unit the agent/step within a trial, validated causally via counterfactual replay.

We apply a targeted edit at the implicated location (message, plan, tool call), and rerun the system, judging success by milestone/utility gains.

HYPOTHESIS-TO-BE-TESTED / contrast with log-based (exact match): "these attributions are inferred from logs and remain an untested hypothesis unless validated by execution. In DoVer, we treat attribution as a hypothesis to be tested. We apply a targeted edit at the implicated location (message, plan, tool call), and rerun the system, judging success by milestone/utility gains."

claim: DoVer is an intervention-driven (Do-then-Verify) auto-debugging pipeline that, unlike pure log-based attribution, treats failure attribution as a hypothesis to be tested: for each 'trial' it hypothesizes a faulty step or agent, turns that into a targeted edit (modified orchestrator instructions to sub-agents, or plan updates), and re-executes/replays the trajectory in-situ to verify whether the hypothesis is supported or refuted. output.evidence:
Verified against the primary PDF (arXiv:2512.06749v3, "DoVer: Intervention-Driven Auto Debugging for LLM Multi-Agent Systems"), text extracted via pdftotext. The claim mixes supported facts with at least one outright factual error and one figure the paper's own results refute.

(1) SUPPORTED parts: "validates or refutes 30-60% of failure hypotheses" — confirmed by abstract (line 34), intro (line 85), and Table 3 (combined validated+refuted = 29.2% WW-AB, 37.4% WW-GAIA, 58.7% GAIA-Level-1). "49% flip rate on AG2/GSMPlus generalization" — confirmed by intro (line 88: "achieving a 49% flip rate on failure cases") and Table 2 (GSMPlus = 49.0% over 198 trials). "Flip rate" is defined (line 394-395) as Trial Success Rate = "ratio of trial runs that successfully complete the task after intervention."

(2) FACTUAL ERROR (unambiguous): The claim's parenthetical "(~27.5% reported on GSMPlus trials in Table 2)" is FALSE. Table 2 reports GSMPlus at 49.0%, NOT 27.5%. The 27.5% figure in Table 2 belongs to GAIA-Level-1 (63 trials), a different dataset/setting — confirmed by §5.2 prose (line 517-518): "Trial Success Rate across all intervened trials is 17.6% for cases in WW, compared to a higher success rate of 27.5% for GAIA-Level-1 cases." The claim mislabels GSMPlus as both 49% AND ~27.5% — internally contradictory.

(3) OVERREACH refuted by the paper's own table: The claim's "28% (GAIA)" is taken from the abstract (line 33) / intro (line 84: "recovers 18% and 28% of failures on... AssistantBench... and GAIA, respectively"), but this 28% is NOT reproduced anywhere in the results. Table 2 shows WW-GAIA = 17.6% (not 28%), and line 602 explicitly states "DoVer recovers 17.6% of trials" for GAIA. §5.2 confirms all WW cases (AB + GAIA) = 17.6%. The only ~28% value in the entire results section is GAIA-Level-1's 27.5%, a separate held-out subset (45 extra Level-1 cases from GAIA's validation set, line 478), not the main GAIA result. So the headline "28% on GAIA" is contradicted by the paper's own Table 2/§5.2.

Net: while DoVer's repair improvements are real, the claim as written contains a clear misattribution (27.5%→GSMPlus, should be GAIA-Level-1) and propagates an abstract figure (28% GAIA) that the body data (17.6%) does not support.

claim: A2P is a counterfactual/causal-intervention attribution algorithm operationalizing Pearl's structural causal model: it (1) Abducts hidden root causes behind an agent's action, (2) Acts by defining a minimal corrective intervention via the do()-operator, and (3) Predicts by simulating the counterfactual trajectory (3-5 turns) to verify whether correcting that single action would have averted the failure — distinguishing decisive errors from incidental ones. It runs within a single inference pass as an enhancement of the All-at-Once method (retaining full-context access).
quote: A2P guides an LLM through a formal, three-step counterfactual reasoning process within a single inference pass, operationalizing the logic of Pearl's structural causal model hierarchy ... (1) Abduct, inferring hidden factors ... (2) Act, defining a minimal, concrete corrective intervention; and (3) Predict, simulating the subsequent counterfactual trajectory to verify if the intervention would have resolved the overall task failure ... Our approach is implemented as an enhancement to the All-at-Once method, thereby retaining its key advantage of having access to the complete conversational context.

output.evidence: Every component of the claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source (arXiv 2509.10401, "Abduct, Act, Predict," Weng/Zhu/Lin/Ning/Zhang, Westlake U., Sept 2025), confirmed across independent fetches of the abstract and full HTML. (1) Pearl's SCM: "operationalizing the logic of Pearl's structural causal model hierarchy." (2) Abduct: eq. "ϵt ← arg max_ϵ P(ϵ|s₀:t, aₜ, Z(τ)=1)"; "inferring hidden factors (e.g., a flawed assumption) that explain a problematic action." (3) Act + do()-operator: "This corresponds to applying the do()-operator in Pearl's causal calculus"; eq. "do(aₜ ← aₜ*)"; "minimal, concrete corrective intervention." (4) Predict 3-5 turns: "The LLM is instructed to simulate the subsequent 3-5 turns of the conversation under the counterfactual condition"; sensitivity analysis confirms the 3-5 range is optimal. (5) Decisive vs incidental: "directly evaluates the decisive nature of the error"; "distinguish decisive errors from incidental mistakes." (6) Single pass + All-at-Once: "within a single inference pass"; "implemented as an enhancement to the All-at-Once method, thereby retaining its key advantage of having access to the complete conversational context." The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim paraphrase of the method's self-description — no overreach. Minor nuance (not a refutation): the "Predict" step is the LLM SIMULATING a trajectory in-context, not a real re-execution/intervention on a live system; the claim correctly uses "simulating," matching the paper. Source-quality caveat: the paper self-declares ~95% AI-authored ("DeepScientist"), which undercuts confidence in its EMPIRICAL results (47.46% AG / 29.31% HC step accuracy) but NOT in this purely descriptive claim about what the method is and how it is framed. The research question itself lists "tool-call step" as a valid "node" form and counts "counterfactual replay of a node" under question #3, so the claim's narrow assertion ("interventional counterfactual attribution, directly relevant to counterfactual blame-assignment approaches") holds; it does NOT claim node/agent-level multi-agent attribution. claim: The paper develops and evaluates three distinct automated failure-attribution algorithms — All-at-Once (whole-trace single query), Step-by-Step (incremental sequential analysis), and Binary Search (recursive divide-and-conquer over the trace) — which directly map to the algorithm taxonomy in the research question.
quote: All-at-Once: "The model is provided with the entire task execution trace and asked to identify which agent caused the failure and at which step." ... Binary Search: "The model divides the execution trace into halves, queries whether failure occurred in each segment, and recursively narrows down to identify the responsible step and agent."

output.evidence: VERIFIED against the primary PDF (arXiv 2505.00212v3, extracted locally via PyMuPDF). The paper's Section 3/4.1 defines exactly three methods verbatim: (1) "All-at-once: An LLM is provided with a query and the complete failure log, and it is tasked with identifying the failure-responsible agent as well as the specific step where the decisive error occurred" = whole-trace single query. (2) "Step-by-step: ...the failure log is presented step-by-step. At each step, the LLM is tasked with determining whether a mistake has occurred in the current step... Otherwise, the process continues until the final step is reached" = incremental sequential analysis. (3) "Binary search: ...the LLM is tasked with determining whether the mistake occurred in the upper half or lower half of the failure logs. Once this decision is made, the LLM is provided with the selected half of the log and the process is repeated iteratively until a single step is identified" = divide-and-conquer over halves. The paper consistently calls these "three automated failure attribution methods" / "three algorithms" / "three baseline methods" (abstract, Table 1, Figures 3-4), and Figure 3 labels them "All-at-Once / Step-by-Step / Binary Search." The research question's own taxonomy ("all-at-once vs step-by-step vs binary-search vs causal/counterfactual") names these three verbatim, so the mapping is direct and exact. Independent corroboration: Synced (PSU/Duke writeup) and multiple search results list the same three. Adversarial nitpicks that do NOT refute: (a) the claim says Binary Search is "recursive" while the paper says "repeated iteratively" — immaterial, since binary-search-over-halves is the canonical divide-and-conquer algorithm and iterative-vs-recursive is an implementation detail; the parenthetical "divide-and-conquer over the trace" is accurate. (b) The claim's "supporting quote" paraphrases ("entire task execution trace" vs the paper's "complete failure log"; reconstructed wording for Binary Search) but the substance is faithful to the verified primary text and not misleading. Source quality is maximal (primary source, ICML 2025 Spotlight, open-source). Not outdated (2025). No contradicting source found.
